item.title: 10 productivity tools that all students need
item.itunes:title: 10 productivity tools that all students need
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • TickTick: A must-have productivity bundle for task management, calendar integration, and theming.
  • Grammarly: Improve your writing with AI assistance, typo and grammar checks, and plagiarism detection.
  • Snagit: A feature-rich alternative to the Snipping Tool for advanced screenshot and screen recording capabilities.

As a student, juggling tight project deadlines, limited finances, assignments, tasks, exams, and social life can feel like a high-wire act. Thankfully, there is no shortage of productivity tools to help you sail through challenging situations. Among them, we have hand-picked the top game-changing apps to completely transform the way you study, collaborate with classmates, handle projects, and manage your time on your laptop.

2:58
Related
10 essential apps you should install on your new PC

Just got a new Windows PC? Here are 10 apps you should install to help you make the most of it.

10 TickTick

A must-have productivity bundle

TickTick is one of the best Microsoft To-Do alternatives out there. Aside from task management, it excels in other areas, too. For instance, it comes with robust calendar integration to sync your Google or Outlook calendar, and shows upcoming classes with just a single click.

TickTick has nailed the theming engine with different themes based on major cities worldwide. It also supports a popular Pomodoro technique with short breaks, a dedicated habit tracker, and more. TickTick also lets you convert task lists into Kanban boards for your small projects. TickTick premium plans start at $4 per month.

TickTick
See at TickTick

9 Grammarly

An AI assistant to improve your writing

Grammarly is another must-have tool for students. Whether you are writing a report, assignment, email, or even creating a presentation, typos and grammar mistakes do leave a bad impression. Here’s where Grammarly’s excellent AI writing assistant comes into play.

Grammarly shows your overall writing score based on word count, readability, and vocabulary. It displays suggestions to fix your typos, grammar, and punctuation, and helps you rephrase sentences for a better tone. Grammarly can also check for plagiarism and make sure your report is original before final submission. Grammarly has native apps on all platforms, an extension for Chrome, Edge, and Safari, and is also available as an add-on in Microsoft 365 apps. Grammarly is free to download and use. Some features like plagiarism checker are locked behind a $12 per month paywall.

Grammarly
See at Chrome Web Store

8 Snagit

A feature-rich Snipping Tool alternative

Although Microsoft has improved the Snipping Tool with frequent updates, it still leaves a lot to be desired for power users. If you frequently take screenshots and record your screen, Snagit is a no-brainer choice for you.

Snagit supports scrolling screenshots and text recognition, and also comes with a powerful editor to add arrows, text, callouts, shapes, stamps, blur effects, and more to your media files. One of the best Snagit features is presets, where you can define certain conditions that automatically apply after you take a screenshot or record a video. Snagit is priced $63 as one-time purchase and comes with one year of free maintenance.

Snagit by TechSmith
See at Snagit

7 OneNote

The ultimate note-taking tool

Your academic years are simply incomplete without a capable note-taking app. While there are dozens of note-taking solutions out there, OneNote remains the ideal pick for students and educators. First of all, OneNote is free to download and use for local use. Additionally, as the oldest software out there, OneNote is packed with a long list of features.

Speaking of some of the noteworthy add-ons, OneNote has audio recording with live transcription to help you during long lectures, robust stylus support to jot down notes, different paper styles, a capable note organization system, support for math equations, and more.

If OneNote isn’t your cup of tea, check our separate post to find other top note taking alternatives.

Microsoft OneNote
See at Microsoft Store

6 Toshl

Keep your expenses in check

Tracking your daily and recurring expenses is another key part during your college days. As a student, you won’t want to overspend on things you don’t need. Among all the expense manager apps, Toshl easily stands out for several reasons.

Toshl was designed to be your personal finance app, packed with useful add-ons like a monthly spending review, budgets, locations, an overview to check out your expenses and income, reminders, graphs to analyze data, and more. Toshl also supports secure integration with bank accounts, credit cards, and other financial accounts to import data automatically. Toshl has native apps on iPhone, Android, and the web. Toshl Pro starts at just $3 per month.

Toshl
See at Toshl

5 Microsoft Copilot

A handy companion during extensive research

Copilot is a ChatGPT-powered AI chatbot built into Windows. With Copilot, you can enjoy all the benefits of ChatGPT-4 without opting for any paid plan. Copilot works well within the Microsoft 365 ecosystem. Copilot supports plugins where you can choose up to three plugins per conversation and receive data from your favorite services like Instacart, KAYAK, OpenTable, and more.

You can use Copilot for your complex projects and presentations, and even ask it to draft a professional email on your behalf. It definitely cuts down your research hours and remains our go-to recommendation for efficiency.

Microsoft Copilot
See at Copilot

4 Google Drive

Access your files on all devices

Whether you want to store media files, documents, presentations, class spreadsheets, or project PDFs, Google Drive can be your ideal cloud storage solution to get the job done. It comes with 15GB of free storage and has seamless integration with other apps like Meet, Tasks, Keep, and more.

Google Drive recently received a Material You makeover on the web, which comes with a neat Home view to see all of your frequently used files. Google Drive paid plans start at $2 per month for 100GB. Check our separate guide if you want to learn more about the differences between Google Drive, OneDrive, Dropbox, and Nextcloud.

Google Drive
Starts at $2/month

3 Notion

A capable digital closet

Notion is your ultimate database tool to track everything related to your studies and personal life. It uses a modern block editor and comes with a range of options to create powerful databases and pages. You can use different database views, master Notion AI to change the tone, improve writing, compose a creative story, and more. You can also explore integration with third-party apps, so that you can view relevant content within your Notion page.

Notion has a rich template library to help you get started drafting a page in no time. You can use a travel planner, create a school life dashboard or habit tracker, manage your group assignments, class notes, course schedule, and much more with ready-to-use templates. Notion pricing starts at $8 per month.

Notion
See at Notion

2 Toggl Track

Track your time like a pro

As a student, you need to track your study hours efficiently. You can use a software solution like Toggl Track to keep a close eye on how much time you spend on your desktop. It supports automated time tracking, works with popular apps and services, and has timesheet reports for detailed analysis.

While there are many usecase for Toggl Track, students can mostly use it for project time tracking. It also helps you develop good habits when you move from your college days to your professional life. Toggl Track has a solid free plan on offer. The paid plans start at $9 per month. Toggl Track may feel overkill at first glance, but it's still worth trying out if you need some feedback towards better time-management.

Toggl Track
See at Toggl

1 Canva

A robust graphic design tool

With the recent updates, Canva has grown beyond a graphic design tool. It now supports docs, whiteboards, and presentations, and even lets you create a website from scratch. Whether you want to create stunning infographics, photo collages, mind maps, sign-up forms, or a simple Instagram post, head to the Canva template library and start your design in seconds.

Canva also has a robust collection of resumes to choose from. Instead of spending hours in Word, you can simply use a Canva template and create an attention-worthy CV in minutes. Thanks to Canva’s robust editor, AI tools, and seamless sharing, you won’t miss Adobe apps on your laptop. Canva Pro starts at $10 per month.

Canva
See at Canva

Complete your college years in style

From smart note-taking apps to time-tracking tools, these software solutions are tailor-made for student success. You don’t need to download and use all the apps in the list above. Just pick a few of the most relevant ones based on your needs, and manage your study hours like a pro.

If you want to explore more tools like this, check our general post about other top productivity apps.

Microsoft OneNote
See at Microsoft Store

6 Toshl

Keep your expenses in check

Tracking your daily and recurring expenses is another key part during your college days. As a student, you won’t want to overspend on things you don’t need. Among all the expense manager apps, Toshl easily stands out for several reasons.

Toshl was designed to be your personal finance app, packed with useful add-ons like a monthly spending review, budgets, locations, an overview to check out your expenses and income, reminders, graphs to analyze data, and more. Toshl also supports secure integration with bank accounts, credit cards, and other financial accounts to import data automatically. Toshl has native apps on iPhone, Android, and the web. Toshl Pro starts at just $3 per month.

Toshl
See at Toshl

5 Microsoft Copilot

A handy companion during extensive research

Copilot is a ChatGPT-powered AI chatbot built into Windows. With Copilot, you can enjoy all the benefits of ChatGPT-4 without opting for any paid plan. Copilot works well within the Microsoft 365 ecosystem. Copilot supports plugins where you can choose up to three plugins per conversation and receive data from your favorite services like Instacart, KAYAK, OpenTable, and more.

You can use Copilot for your complex projects and presentations, and even ask it to draft a professional email on your behalf. It definitely cuts down your research hours and remains our go-to recommendation for efficiency.

Microsoft Copilot
See at Copilot

4 Google Drive

Access your files on all devices

Whether you want to store media files, documents, presentations, class spreadsheets, or project PDFs, Google Drive can be your ideal cloud storage solution to get the job done. It comes with 15GB of free storage and has seamless integration with other apps like Meet, Tasks, Keep, and more.

Google Drive recently received a Material You makeover on the web, which comes with a neat Home view to see all of your frequently used files. Google Drive paid plans start at $2 per month for 100GB. Check our separate guide if you want to learn more about the differences between Google Drive, OneDrive, Dropbox, and Nextcloud.

Google Drive
Starts at $2/month

3 Notion

A capable digital closet

Notion is your ultimate database tool to track everything related to your studies and personal life. It uses a modern block editor and comes with a range of options to create powerful databases and pages. You can use different database views, master Notion AI to change the tone, improve writing, compose a creative story, and more. You can also explore integration with third-party apps, so that you can view relevant content within your Notion page.

Notion has a rich template library to help you get started drafting a page in no time. You can use a travel planner, create a school life dashboard or habit tracker, manage your group assignments, class notes, course schedule, and much more with ready-to-use templates. Notion pricing starts at $8 per month.

Notion
See at Notion

2 Toggl Track

Track your time like a pro

As a student, you need to track your study hours efficiently. You can use a software solution like Toggl Track to keep a close eye on how much time you spend on your desktop. It supports automated time tracking, works with popular apps and services, and has timesheet reports for detailed analysis.

While there are many usecase for Toggl Track, students can mostly use it for project time tracking. It also helps you develop good habits when you move from your college days to your professional life. Toggl Track has a solid free plan on offer. The paid plans start at $9 per month. Toggl Track may feel overkill at first glance, but it's still worth trying out if you need some feedback towards better time-management.

Toggl Track
See at Toggl

1 Canva

A robust graphic design tool

With the recent updates, Canva has grown beyond a graphic design tool. It now supports docs, whiteboards, and presentations, and even lets you create a website from scratch. Whether you want to create stunning infographics, photo collages, mind maps, sign-up forms, or a simple Instagram post, head to the Canva template library and start your design in seconds.

Canva also has a robust collection of resumes to choose from. Instead of spending hours in Word, you can simply use a Canva template and create an attention-worthy CV in minutes. Thanks to Canva’s robust editor, AI tools, and seamless sharing, you won’t miss Adobe apps on your laptop. Canva Pro starts at $10 per month.

Canva
See at Canva

Complete your college years in style

From smart note-taking apps to time-tracking tools, these software solutions are tailor-made for student success. You don’t need to download and use all the apps in the list above. Just pick a few of the most relevant ones based on your needs, and manage your study hours like a pro.

If you want to explore more tools like this, check our general post about other top productivity apps.
item.content:encoded:
Key Takeaways

  • TickTick: A must-have productivity bundle for task management, calendar integration, and theming.
  • Grammarly: Improve your writing with AI assistance, typo and grammar checks, and plagiarism detection.
  • Snagit: A feature-rich alternative to the Snipping Tool for advanced screenshot and screen recording capabilities.

As a student, juggling tight project deadlines, limited finances, assignments, tasks, exams, and social life can feel like a high-wire act. Thankfully, there is no shortage of productivity tools to help you sail through challenging situations. Among them, we have hand-picked the top game-changing apps to completely transform the way you study, collaborate with classmates, handle projects, and manage your time on your laptop.

2:58
Related
10 essential apps you should install on your new PC

Just got a new Windows PC? Here are 10 apps you should install to help you make the most of it.

10 TickTick

A must-have productivity bundle

TickTick is one of the best Microsoft To-Do alternatives out there. Aside from task management, it excels in other areas, too. For instance, it comes with robust calendar integration to sync your Google or Outlook calendar, and shows upcoming classes with just a single click.

TickTick has nailed the theming engine with different themes based on major cities worldwide. It also supports a popular Pomodoro technique with short breaks, a dedicated habit tracker, and more. TickTick also lets you convert task lists into Kanban boards for your small projects. TickTick premium plans start at $4 per month.

TickTick
See at TickTick

9 Grammarly

An AI assistant to improve your writing

Grammarly is another must-have tool for students. Whether you are writing a report, assignment, email, or even creating a presentation, typos and grammar mistakes do leave a bad impression. Here’s where Grammarly’s excellent AI writing assistant comes into play.

Grammarly shows your overall writing score based on word count, readability, and vocabulary. It displays suggestions to fix your typos, grammar, and punctuation, and helps you rephrase sentences for a better tone. Grammarly can also check for plagiarism and make sure your report is original before final submission. Grammarly has native apps on all platforms, an extension for Chrome, Edge, and Safari, and is also available as an add-on in Microsoft 365 apps. Grammarly is free to download and use. Some features like plagiarism checker are locked behind a $12 per month paywall.

Grammarly
See at Chrome Web Store

8 Snagit

A feature-rich Snipping Tool alternative

Although Microsoft has improved the Snipping Tool with frequent updates, it still leaves a lot to be desired for power users. If you frequently take screenshots and record your screen, Snagit is a no-brainer choice for you.

Snagit supports scrolling screenshots and text recognition, and also comes with a powerful editor to add arrows, text, callouts, shapes, stamps, blur effects, and more to your media files. One of the best Snagit features is presets, where you can define certain conditions that automatically apply after you take a screenshot or record a video. Snagit is priced $63 as one-time purchase and comes with one year of free maintenance.

Snagit by TechSmith
See at Snagit

7 OneNote

The ultimate note-taking tool

Your academic years are simply incomplete without a capable note-taking app. While there are dozens of note-taking solutions out there, OneNote remains the ideal pick for students and educators. First of all, OneNote is free to download and use for local use. Additionally, as the oldest software out there, OneNote is packed with a long list of features.

Speaking of some of the noteworthy add-ons, OneNote has audio recording with live transcription to help you during long lectures, robust stylus support to jot down notes, different paper styles, a capable note organization system, support for math equations, and more.

If OneNote isn’t your cup of tea, check our separate post to find other top note taking alternatives.

Microsoft OneNote
See at Microsoft Store

Keep your expenses in check

Toshl also supports secure integration with bank accounts, credit cards, and other financial accounts to import data automatically. Toshl has native apps on iPhone, Android, and the web. Toshl Pro starts at just $3 per month.

Toshl
See at Toshl

item.title: Microsoft's response to the distaste with ads in Windows 11 is to cram even more into the Weather app
item.itunes:title: Microsoft's response to the distaste with ads in Windows 11 is to cram even more into the Weather app
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Microsoft adds ads to Windows 11 Weather app, sparking user backlash.
  • Users respond to the addition of ads with ad-blocking tools and an unofficial Windows 11 ad blocker.
  • The ongoing battle between Microsoft and its users continues over ad placement.

Microsoft's relation between itself and its users have been turbulent over the years. Some days, Microsoft listens to its users and reverts any changes that its users really don't like. This time, however, it seems the Redmond giant is putting its foot down over ads in Windows 11. After we saw ads appear in the Start menu and Game Pass recommendations in the Settings menu, Microsoft is continuing its ad-based quest by adding ads to the Weather app.

Related
Start11 v2 review: Making the Windows 11 Start menu actually useful

With tons of configuration options for appearance and organization, Start11 makes the Start menu and taskbar so much richer

The Weather app gets ads on Windows 11

As spotted by Tom's Hardware, Microsoft is beginning its first wave of ads in the Weather app. This was first spotted by @ChangeWindows on X, who keeps people up-to-date on what's arriving in the newest Windows patch. In their post, they had some choice words about the addition:

@ChangeWindows made another post stating that they did think that the update itself was great and added some much-needed features. However, they noted that the update was "used to add more ads" and that there were a lot of minor things that weren't properly fleshed out with the update.

The battle between Microsoft and its own users wages on

Given that user feedback against the ads isn't having an effect, users are taking matters into their own hands. For example, when the Start menu ads first arrived, we saw a new Windows 11 ad control tool and an unofficial Windows 11 ad blocker arrive within a day of one another. As such, Microsoft will likely have a fight on its hands as users find ways to block the ads and use the operating system as they desire.

item.title: Microsoft is already killing everything I love about Copilot
item.itunes:title: Microsoft is already killing everything I love about Copilot
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Microsoft transformed Copilot into a PWA, losing system-level integration and sidebar functionality.
  • Copilot's unique features that set it apart from competitors like ChatGPT and Gemini are now gone.
  • Microsoft's Copilot+ laptops debut with limited functionality, raising questions about the brand's value.

Microsoft quietly made a big change to the way Copilot works on Windows 11 PCs, including on brand-new Copilot+ laptops. Since its inception, Microsoft Copilot was an artificial intelligence-based chatbot that could also tap into your PC. Copilot could leverage OpenAI's large language models (LLMs), but it could also help you turn settings like Wi-Fi or Bluetooth on or off. However, it's losing a key part of its functionality with a recent update. The dedicated Copilot key on Windows laptops and the Copilot icon in the taskbar now launch a progressive web app (PWA) version of Copilot.

Related
3 things I learned using the Copilot key on laptops for a week

It might be months or years before you daily drive a laptop with a Copilot key. Let's find out what it's like.

PWAs essentially mimic the experience of a website in windowed form, and do not function like a native Windows 11 app. By turning Copilot into a PWA, it's unable to be used as a Windows 11 sidebar or with system-level integration. When I tested my first laptop with a Copilot key earlier this year, I thought the chatbot would be most useful to casual users as tech support. Who needs to waste time on the phone if the Copilot sidebar could find the setting or problem with your PC that needs changing?

That's why I'm so disappointed with Microsoft's decision to strip Copilot of all the functionality that made it great. As a PWA, Copilot is no different from competitors ChatGPT or Gemini. And, the Copilot key is just a fancy macro.

Copilot is now just a PWA

The sidebar that was deeply integrated with your PC is no more

With how hard Microsoft is pushing Copilot, it's easy to forget that the feature is still in preview. The company says that it is "evolving the Copilot experience on Windows" based on user feedback.The release of Windows 11 Insider Preview Build 22635.3785 includes Copilot changing from a sidebar chatbot into a PWA with limited features. Here's how Microsoft explains the changes:

As part of this update, we’re also evolving the Copilot experience on Windows as an app that will be pinned to the taskbar. This enables users to get the benefits of a traditional app experience, including the ability to resize, move, and snap the window – feedback we’ve heard from users throughout the preview of Copilot in Windows. This model also allows Microsoft to more agilely develop and optimize the experience based on user feedback.

Separately, the company is removing the WIN + C keyboard shortcut that was previously used to trigger Copilot. It's true that there are clear benefits to using Copilot in windowed form. It's also true that, as a PWA, it's easier for Microsoft to make quick updates to Copilot on the fly. As for the part about responding to feedback from Windows 11 users, I'm not so sure that the ability to resize a window was high enough on their requested feature list that made its addition worth removing core functionality from Copilot.

Copilot+ is moving backward, not forward

Microsoft already recalled Recall, and now Copilot isn't as good

If you remember, Microsoft mandated that laptops supporting Copilot+ must have a dedicated Copilot key. However, that key isn't even necessary now that the Copilot PWA is pinned to the Windows 11 taskbar by default. It's also worth mentioning that Copilot is the namesake of Microsoft's Copilot+ branding, and yet it's losing functionality right as new Copilot+ laptops debut — like the company's own Surface Laptop 7.

Related
Surface Laptop 7 15 review: You don't have to wait for Windows on Arm to get good anymore

It does most things right

Copilot+ was already underwhelming, since Microsoft pulled the brand's key feature — Recall — due to serious privacy concerns. Now, the release of Copilot+ coincides with Copilot itself losing features. Somehow, Microsoft managed to make a big moment for Windows on Arm underwhelming thanks to multiple Copilot blunders.

What makes Copilot unique now?

The key is no better than a shortcut for ChatGPT or Gemini

There are numerous AI chatbots that users can turn to, but there was something special about the old Copilot. It wasn't just a chatbot that could tap into the web and various LLMs. It was also a portal to your PC itself, helping you make changes and settings tweaks that you might not have been able to do on your own. For example, you could tell Copilot to change your network settings. Then, it would show you what it planned to modify and present you with Yes and No buttons. Clicking one of those options was all it took to change a setting on your PC with Copilot.

I thought that was Copilot's "killer feature" that would separate the chatbot from all the others. Now, as a PWA, Copilot is just a carbon-copy of the other AI chatbots available. It uses the same GPT-4o model from OpenAI as ChatGPT, and it's a PWA just like Google's Gemini on ChromeOS. There's nothing unique about this version of Copilot, and that's strange, because Microsoft clearly wants Copilot to be a key part of its AI PC branding moving forward.

Related
ChatGPT vs Microsoft Copilot vs Google Gemini: What are the differences?

If you've been trying to figure out which generative AI tool is better, you've come to the right place

Here's how Microsoft explains the changes:

As part of this update, we’re also evolving the Copilot experience on Windows as an app that will be pinned to the taskbar. This enables users to get the benefits of a traditional app experience, including the ability to resize, move, and snap the window – feedback we’ve heard from users throughout the preview of Copilot in Windows. This model also allows Microsoft to more agilely develop and optimize the experience based on user feedback.

Separately, the company is removing the WIN + C keyboard shortcut that was previously used to trigger Copilot. It's true that there are clear benefits to using Copilot in windowed form. It's also true that, as a PWA, it's easier for Microsoft to make quick updates to Copilot on the fly. As for the part about responding to feedback from Windows 11 users, I'm not so sure that the ability to resize a window was high enough on their requested feature list that made its addition worth removing core functionality from Copilot.

Copilot+ is moving backward, not forward

Microsoft already recalled Recall, and now Copilot isn't as good

If you remember, Microsoft mandated that laptops supporting Copilot+ must have a dedicated Copilot key. However, that key isn't even necessary now that the Copilot PWA is pinned to the Windows 11 taskbar by default. It's also worth mentioning that Copilot is the namesake of Microsoft's Copilot+ branding, and yet it's losing functionality right as new Copilot+ laptops debut — like the company's own Surface Laptop 7.

Surface Laptop 7 15 review: You don't have to wait for Windows on Arm to get good anymore

It does most things right

Copilot+ was already underwhelming, since Microsoft pulled the brand's key feature — Recall — due to serious privacy concerns. Now, the release of Copilot+ coincides with Copilot itself losing features. Somehow, Microsoft managed to make a big moment for Windows on Arm underwhelming thanks to multiple Copilot blunders.

What makes Copilot unique now?

The key is no better than a shortcut for ChatGPT or Gemini

There are numerous AI chatbots that users can turn to, but there was something special about the old Copilot. It wasn't just a chatbot that could tap into the web and various LLMs. It was also a portal to your PC itself, helping you make changes and settings tweaks that you might not have been able to do on your own. For example, you could tell Copilot to change your network settings. Then, it would show you what it planned to modify and present you with Yes and No buttons. Clicking one of those options was all it took to change a setting on your PC with Copilot.

I thought that was Copilot's "killer feature" that would separate the chatbot from all the others. Now, as a PWA, Copilot is just a carbon-copy of the other AI chatbots available. It uses the same GPT-4o model from OpenAI as ChatGPT, and it's a PWA just like Google's Gemini on ChromeOS. There's nothing unique about this version of Copilot, and that's strange, because Microsoft clearly wants Copilot to be a key part of its AI PC branding moving forward.
